output_text:
Cleaned the prose and flagged a load-bearing contradiction between the "training-free / no supervision" framing and the described pairwise-ranking training on labeled RouterBench data.

---

**Critical issue to resolve before any further polish**

The section makes two incompatible claims:

- Paragraph 1: EcoRoute is "training-free," "requires no routing-specific supervision," and is "immediately deployable in any new domain without a data-collection phase."
- Paragraph 3: the scoring head is trained on 50,000 labeled (prompt, model_a, model_b) triples from RouterBench, using GPT-4-as-judge quality annotations — i.e., exactly routing-specific supervision.
- Paragraph 6 then attributes the empirical gains to "the absence of any labeling burden," which directly contradicts paragraph 3.

You need to pick one story. Either (a) the scoring head is trained on RouterBench labels — in which case "training-free" and "no labeling burden" must come out, and the deployment-without-data-collection claim must be softened to "the trained router transfers zero-shot to new domains"; or (b) the method really is label-free — in which case paragraph 3 must be replaced with whatever the actual unsupervised/self-supervised objective is. Below I assume (a), since the experimental section presupposes a trained head.

---

**Revised text (assuming option a)**

We present EcoRoute, a framework for routing user queries across a heterogeneous pool of large language models. Like prior work such as FrugalGPT and RouteLLM, EcoRoute learns to predict which model in the pool will best serve a given prompt; unlike those systems, it is designed so that the same trained router transfers to new deployments without per-domain re-labeling. All routing decisions are made from features of the prompt alone, before any candidate LLM is queried.

The router operates in three stages. An embedding model maps the prompt to a fixed-dimensional vector; a lightweight scoring head produces a per-model utility estimate; and the model that maximizes utility minus cost is selected and forwarded the prompt.

We train the scoring head on the RouterBench training split, in which each prompt is paired with responses from every candidate LLM and a GPT-4-as-judge quality score. We sample 50,000 (prompt, model_a, model_b) triples and minimize a pairwise logistic loss that penalizes incorrect orderings. Training converges in roughly four A100-GPU-hours.

At inference time the scoring head is fixed and adds negligible latency (~3 ms per prompt on a CPU host). Because no candidate LLM is queried until routing completes, the routing overhead is independent of pool size — a property we call *model-pool agnosticism*.

We compare EcoRoute against three baselines: a uniform random router, a cost-only router, and the cascade router from FrugalGPT. On the RouterBench evaluation split, EcoRoute reaches 92% of GPT-4's average quality at 18% of its cost, beating the cascade by 7 quality points at matched cost.

---

**Other recommendations**

1. Drop the final-paragraph causal claim ("we attribute these gains to the absence of any labeling burden"). The comparison is to baselines that also use no additional labels at deployment time, so the labeling-burden axis doesn't explain the quality gap. If you want a mechanistic attribution, point at the pairwise ranking objective or the prompt-only feature set.
2. "model-pool agnosticism" is a strong term for "routing cost is O(1) in pool size." Consider a more literal name (e.g., "pool-size-invariant routing") unless agnosticism is a term you're deliberately establishing.
3. The 92%/18% headline number needs a confidence interval or a note on variance across the eval split; a single point estimate against three baselines is thin for the claim.
4. Specify the embedding model in this section or forward-reference §X — readers will want to know whether the gains are coming from the embedding or the head.
5. "negligible latency overhead (~3ms per prompt on a CPU host)" — say which CPU; 3 ms on a Xeon vs. an M2 is a 3× difference and reviewers will ask.

---

**Uncertainty**: I have not verified RouterBench's exact licensing or whether the 50k-triple sampling scheme matches a published protocol; recommendation (3) assumes RouterBench's eval split is large enough to support a CI but I haven't checked its size.
